plainMeaning: These two Jinas shine by their own innate nobility — flawless, supreme in virtue, serene, strengthened by penance, loved by glory, and honored by the sages.
recitation: sahāva-lakkhā samappaïṭṭhā, adosa-duṭṭhā guṇehiṁ jiṭṭhā, pasāya siṭṭhā taveṇa puṭṭhā, sirīhiṁ iṭṭhā, risīhiṁ juṭṭhā.

wordByWord.gloss: marked/distinguished by their own innate nature
wordByWord.etymology: Skt svabhāva ('own, innate nature') + lakṣaṇa ('distinguishing mark') → Pkt sahāva + lakkhā; alternatively svabhāva-lakṣya, 'recognizable by their very nature'.
wordByWord.notes: Opening epithet: their excellence is not borrowed but constitutive — they bear nobility as their natural lakṣaṇa.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt sam(yak)-pratiṣṭhita / sampratiṣṭhā ('well set up, firmly fixed'); Pkt -païṭṭha = pratiṣṭhita, pp. of pra-√sthā.

wordByWord.gloss: untainted by fault, uncorrupted by any defect
wordByWord.etymology: Skt a- ('not') + doṣa ('fault, blemish') + duṣṭa ('corrupted, spoiled', pp. of √duṣ) → a-doṣa-duṣṭa.
wordByWord.notes: Double negation intensifies purity: not even touched into corruption by any doṣa.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt guṇaiḥ, instrumental plural of guṇa ('virtue, quality'); Pkt -ehiṁ = Skt -aiḥ.
wordByWord.notes: The book prints this joined to the next word as ગુણેહિંજિટ્ઠા; separated here for clarity.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt jyeṣṭha ('eldest, chief, pre-eminent'); Pkt jiṭṭha.
wordByWord.notes: guṇehiṁ jiṭṭhā = 'supreme by reason of their virtues' — eminence earned, not inherited.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt prasāda ('graciousness, calm clarity, serene favor'); Pkt pasāya, instr. sense.
wordByWord.notes: Prasāda combines outward graciousness with inward tranquil clarity.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt śiṣṭa ('refined, eminent, well-taught') or praśiṣṭa; Pkt siṭṭha.
wordByWord.notes: pasāya siṭṭhā = 'distinguished through serene grace'. A minority reading takes siṭṭha as sṛṣṭa ('emanating'), i.e. 'pouring forth grace'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt tapasā, instrumental of tapas ('austerity, ascetic heat'); Pkt tava.
wordByWord.notes: Sounds the keyword that the very next verse (34) repeats: 'taveṇa dhuya savva pāvayā'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt puṣṭa ('nourished, well-fed, thriving'), pp. of √puṣ; Pkt puṭṭha.
wordByWord.notes: taveṇa puṭṭhā — austerity, which depletes the body, here 'nourishes' the spirit: a deliberate paradox.

wordByWord.gloss: by the Śrīs — splendors / fortunes (instrumental plural)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt śrībhiḥ, instr. pl. of śrī ('glory, majesty, the goddess Lakṣmī'); Pkt sirī.
wordByWord.notes: Plural 'glories' may evoke the personified splendors that attend a Jina.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt iṣṭa ('desired, dear'), pp. of √iṣ; Pkt iṭṭha.
wordByWord.notes: sirīhiṁ iṭṭhā = 'beloved of glory' — splendor itself seeks them.

wordByWord.gloss: by the sages, by the ṛṣis (instrumental plural)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt ṛṣibhiḥ, instr. pl. of ṛṣi ('seer, sage'); Pkt risi.
wordByWord.notes: Balances 'sirīhiṁ' (worldly/celestial glory) with 'risīhiṁ' (ascetic sages) — honored from both quarters.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt juṣṭa ('frequented, served, attended'), pp. of √juṣ; Pkt juṭṭha.
wordByWord.notes: Closes the rhyme-chain (-ṭṭhā): the sages 'frequent' the Jinas as their resort.

literalTranslation: Marked by their own nature, perfectly established, uncorrupted by any fault, supreme through virtues; distinguished by serene grace, nourished by austerity, beloved of the splendors, attended by the sages.
idiomaticTranslation: They bear their greatness as their very nature, firm-founded and wholly unblemished, towering above all by the force of their virtues; serene with grace, made strong through penance, cherished by every splendor and waited upon by the sages themselves.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the preceding stanza paraded the Jinas' auspicious bodily emblems (svastika, bull, lion, wheel), this verse turns inward, replacing outward signs with eight epithets of inner perfection — and it does so as a metrical jewel: every quarter ends in the same -ṭṭhā rhyme (lakkhā, -iṭṭhā, duṭṭhā, jiṭṭhā, siṭṭhā, puṭṭhā, iṭṭhā, juṭṭhā), a chiming cascade that is itself an act of devotional craft. The epithets form a deliberate arc. The first four are intrinsic: their excellence is their own nature (sahāva-lakkhā), firmly grounded (samappaïṭṭhā), untouched by any corruption (adosa-duṭṭhā), and supreme precisely through virtue rather than birth (guṇehiṁ jiṭṭhā). The last four turn relational: graced and serene (pasāya siṭṭhā), strengthened by tapas (taveṇa puṭṭhā), beloved of the splendors (sirīhiṁ iṭṭhā), and waited upon by sages (risīhiṁ juṭṭhā). The closing pair is finely balanced — 'sirīhiṁ' (glory/fortune) against 'risīhiṁ' (renouncing sages): the Jinas are honored at once by the world's splendor and by those who have abandoned it. The pivot word is 'taveṇa puṭṭhā,' 'nourished by austerity' — a calculated paradox, since tapas depletes the body, yet here it feeds the soul. That keyword is no accident: the very next verse opens 'taveṇa dhuya savva pāvayā,' 'by that same austerity all their sins were shaken off' — so this stanza plants 'tapas' as the hinge on which verse 34's promise turns.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes a core Jain conviction: a Jina's supremacy is svabhāva — the soul's own purified nature — not external grant. 'Guṇehiṁ jiṭṭhā' affirms that pre-eminence is won by guṇa, the soul's intrinsic qualities of knowledge, perception, and conduct, while 'adosa-duṭṭhā' points to the destruction of the doṣas (rāga–dveṣa, the kaṣāyas) that the spiritual path targets. 'Taveṇa puṭṭhā' frames tapas not as self-punishment but as the nourishing instrument of nirjarā — the shedding of karma — the doctrinal bridge to the next verse's claim that austerity has shaken off all sin.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For the reciter the verse is a portrait to internalize: greatness is cultivated from within, by guarding against doṣa and by tapas that strengthens rather than merely deprives. Chanting its ringing -ṭṭhā rhyme, one steadies the mind on virtue (guṇa) as the true measure of worth, and is reminded that disciplined austerity is nourishment for the spirit, not punishment of the body.

crossReferences.text: Following verse 34 — 'te taveṇa dhuya savva pāvayā' (purified of all sin by that austerity)
Tattvārtha-sūtra 9.3 — tapas as a means of saṁvara and nirjarā

sources.title: Ajitshanti Stotram (full Prakrit text, scanned)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim verse text and verse position within the hymn; corroboration of the -ṭṭhā rhyming epithet series.

sources.title: Ajit Shanti (અજિત શાંતિ) — sutra lyrics
sources.consultedFor: Cross-checking the Gujarati recitation text of the stava and word boundaries of verse 33.

sources.title: Jyeṣṭha — definitions (Wisdom Library)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit sense of jyeṣṭha ('foremost, supreme') underlying Pkt 'jiṭṭha'.

sources.title: Ajit Shanti Sutra — Jain Sutras and Stotras
sources.consultedFor: General sense-confirmation of the epithet glosses (svabhāva, adoṣa, guṇa, tapas, śrī, ṛṣi).

notes: Orthography preserved verbatim from the book, including the joined form ગુણેહિંજિટ્ઠા (= guṇehiṁ + jiṭṭhā), separated here only for glossing. Two tokens carry well-known variant readings: (a) 'lakkhā' may render either lakṣaṇa ('mark') or lakṣya ('recognizable'), both yielding 'marked/known by their own nature'; (b) 'siṭṭhā' is read here as śiṣṭa ('refined/eminent'), but some traditions read sṛṣṭa ('emanating/pouring forth'), i.e. 'pouring forth grace'. The stanza's striking feature is an end-rhyme on -ṭṭhā in every pāda (a citra/yamaka-style flourish); the exact metrical label is not asserted here. Gloss of the paradoxical 'taveṇa puṭṭhā' ('nourished by austerity') is supported by its deliberate echo in the following verse's 'taveṇa dhuya'.
